monthlyEncouragements.body:
Bring the full tithe into the storehouse, that there may be food in My house, and test Me now in this, says the Lord of Hosts — if I will not open for you the windows of heaven and pour out for you a blessing without end.

The one verse where God says explicitly: come and test Me. With ma'aser.

"Aser te'aser" — our Sages taught: give ma'aser so that you will prosper.

They asked: From where do you know this? He said: Go and test it — as it says, "Test Me now in this."

And what is "without end"? Until your lips wear out from saying "enough."

The salt of money is chaser — reduction. And some say: chesed — kindness.

Rashi: one who wants to "salt" his money — that is, cause it to last — should continually reduce it for tzedakah; and that reduction is its preservation. And those who say chesed: turn it into kindness.

Like salt that preserves food: a little "reduction" for tzedakah is what keeps the money lasting.

If a person sees that their provisions are limited — they should give tzedakah from them, and all the more so when they are plentiful. Whoever "shears" from their assets and gives tzedakah is saved from the judgment of Gehinnom.

A parable of two ewes crossing water: one shorn and one not. The shorn one crossed; the unshorn one got stuck in its soaked wool.

Sometimes what we "shear away" is exactly what lets us get across.

A new immigrant came to the Chazon Ish with a medical problem. The rabbi blessed him and asked him to accept ma'aser from his profits.

After leaving he returned: "I have many expenses — I will tithe only when my situation improves." The Chazon Ish replied: "Already the yetzer is beginning… That is not the way. Accept upon yourself from now to set aside ma'aser properly."

He accepted and was helped. In time he became a major contractor and donor to Torah institutions. When he later stopped tithing — he declined.

King Monobaz spent his treasures and his fathers' treasures in years of famine. His brothers gathered against him: your fathers stored and added — and you waste!

He said: My fathers stored below, and I stored above. They stored money, and I stored lives. They stored for this world, and I stored for the World to Come.

In short: he wasn't wasting. He was transferring to the right account.

The Satmar Rebbe, R. Yoel Teitelbaum zt"l, strongly urged charity — and especially setting aside ma'aser without compromise.

Once at a yeshiva appeal a wealthy man announced: I am giving ten times "chai" — $180. The Rebbe replied: If you had given one "mem-tav" — $440 — you would have a greater mitzvah!

Sometimes the small numbers sound impressive… and the real number is simply bigger.

Just as a person's livelihood is allotted from Rosh Hashanah — so are their losses. If worthy: they give it to tzedakah. If not: others take by force — and even that can count as tzedakah.

Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai dreamed that relatives would lose 700 dinars. Over the year he drew 683 from them for charity. Seventeen remained — and on Erev Yom Kippur the king's men took exactly that.

They asked: why didn't you tell us sooner? We would have given it all! He said: I wanted you to give for its own sake.

Reuven and Shimon were neighbors. Both became wealthy — each from a different remedy.

Reuven heard: honor your wives and you will prosper — he honored his wife with fine clothes and jewelry, and grew rich. Shimon heard: give ma'aser so that you will prosper — he tithed from every profit, and grew rich too.

When Shimon saw that Reuven's spending stayed in his home, while charity money went to the poor — he complained to the sage. The sage answered: don't envy him. Your neighbor merited wealth in this world; you also merited reward in the World to Come. What is given to tzedakah continues with you.

Of Rabbi Shalom Aharon Lopes zt"l of Acre they said: "Rabbi Lopes lives on ma'aser."

Meaning? Often, instead of giving a tenth to tzedakah, only a tenth of his salary remained for himself — and he gave the rest to those in need.

Once people approached him as if he managed a charity fund. He smiled: "Which fund do I manage? The only fund I have is the one of which it is said: and the principal remains for him for the World to Come."

In the home of R. Isser Zalman Meltzer zt"l, a cup fell from the rebbitzin's hand and broke. The rav asked her: perhaps a poor person asked for tzedakah today and you didn't give?

She was astonished — that is exactly what happened in the store: the poor man had no change, and by the time she broke a larger coin he had disappeared.

"I knew it!" declared R. Isser Zalman. "A cup does not break unless it has a good reason."
monthlyEncouragements.source: Story of R. Isser Zalman Meltzer

monthlyEncouragements.body:
They asked the Baal Shem Tov: why do you disperse more than a chomesh — didn't the Sages say "one who wastes should not waste more than a fifth"?

He answered: the word "wastes" (mevazbez) implies plunder — giving without joy, as if robbed. For that, the Sages set a limit. But one who gives with joy and finds pleasure in it — has no limit.

In short: if it feels like waste — keep to a chomesh. If it feels like eternal savings — your heart is already in the right place.
monthlyEncouragements.source: Attributed to the Baal Shem Tov
